title: The Darkness Before Them
author: Matthew Ward
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "The Darkness Before Them" book by Matthew Ward?
Options:
A. “The Eternity King too requires an explanation,” said the thin, darkly handsome man standing opposite Salenna. The lopsided shrug of his heavy scarlet robes oozed self-deprecation, a display of effortless charm Damant would have found more compelling had it not so plainly been rehearsed. Emissaries of the throne were seldom given to spontaneity. For all his cultivated diffidence, Julan Aroth wielded the greatest power of those present, for he reported to the Voice of Caradan Diar – which was as good as speaking directly with the deathless king as made no difference. Indeed, no one had spoken with the Eternity King in living memory – not for many Ages of Fire – for his entire attention was fixed on holding back the all-consuming Veil through force of will. “The king treasures the friendship of Alabastra more highly than gold, and recent events pain him greatly.” 
B. The thin, darkly handsome Julan Aroth, an emissary of the Eternity King, said his master also needed an explanation for the recent events that had greatly distressed the king who valued his friendship with Alabastra highly. Though trying to appear self-deprecating, Julan's rehearsed charm did not sway Damant, for as the Voice of Caradan Diar, Julan had immense power and spoke directly for the deathless king. Indeed, no one had spoken to the Eternity King for many Ages of Fire, since he focused completely on using his will to hold back the all-consuming Veil.
C. The thin and darkly handsome Julan Aroth, an emissary of the Eternity King, said that his master also needed an explanation for recent events, which had greatly pained the king who valued his friendship with Alabastra. Though acting self-deprecating, Julan's rehearsed charm did not sway Damant, for as the Voice of Caradan Diar, Julan wielded immense power and was as good as speaking directly for the deathless king. Indeed, no one had spoken to the Eternity King for many Ages of Fire, as he was focused on using his will to hold back the all-consuming Veil.
D. Julan Aroth, a thin, darkly handsome emissary of the Eternity King, stated that his master also required an explanation for the recent troubling events, which had greatly pained the king who treasured his friendship with Alabastra. Though Julan acted with a rehearsed, self-deprecating charm, it did not convince Damant, for as the Voice of Caradan Diar, Julan held tremendous power and spoke directly for the deathless king. In fact, no one had spoken to the Eternity King for Ages of Fire, as he concentrated fully on using his will to keep the destructive Veil at bay.  
Answer:

The lopsided shrug of his heavy scarlet robes oozed self-deprecation, a display of effortless charm Damant would have found more compelling had it not so plainly been rehearsed. Emissaries of the throne were seldom given to spontaneity. For all his cultivated diffidence, Julan Aroth wielded the greatest power of those present, for he reported to the Voice of Caradan Diar – which was as good as speaking directly with the deathless king as made no difference. Indeed, no one had spoken with the Eternity King in living memory – not for many Ages of Fire – for his entire attention was fixed on holding back the all-consuming Veil through force of will. “The king treasures the friendship of Alabastra more highly than gold, and recent events pain him greatly.”

Though acting self-deprecating, Julan's rehearsed charm did not sway Damant, for as the Voice of Caradan Diar, Julan wielded immense power and was as good as speaking directly for the deathless king.
